facts_disclosed: CDL plus some community college (Miami Dade, business, didnt finish)

turns.content: okay so random question but do you think a college degree actually matters anymore because i was listening to this podcast about self made billionaires and like half of them dropped out of school and it got me thinking about my own situation

That's one of those questions where the answer is genuinely "it depends." A degree still matters for certain fields -- medicine, law, engineering -- but for a lot of careers and especially entrepreneurship, the correlation between a degree and success is way weaker than people assume. What's your own situation that's got you thinking about it?

so after high school in miami i enrolled at miami dade college for business right because i thought okay im in america i should get a degree thats what you do and i liked the classes actually i was pretty good at the accounting stuff and the marketing classes were interesting but the problem was money like always its money

Miami Dade is a solid school, especially for business fundamentals. And liking the classes and being good at accounting -- that's not nothing, a lot of people force themselves through a degree they hate. What happened with the money situation?

i was working two jobs while going to school one at a warehouse in hialeah and one at a gas station on weekends and i was barely covering rent and food let alone tuition and books and my mom was helping when she could but she had her own bills you know and the financial aid covered some of it but not enough and after 3 semesters i just couldnt keep going i was falling asleep in class because i was working until midnight and then up at 7am for lectures and something had to give

Three semesters working two jobs and still not making the math work -- that's not dropping out because you didn't care, that's being forced out by economics. Falling asleep in class after working until midnight isn't a character flaw, it's an impossible schedule. A lot of people hit that same wall at community college. So what came next?

so my moms friend her son was a truck driver and he was making like 50 something thousand a year which at the time sounded like a million dollars to me and he told me about getting a cdl and how the training was only a few weeks and companies would even pay for it if you signed a contract with them so i looked into it and i thought you know what this makes more sense right now than spending 3 more years in school that i cant afford

That's a practical decision, not a failure. CDL training in weeks versus years more of school you couldn't sustain financially, and you come out the other side making real money immediately. Was it hard to make that call at the time, or did it feel like a relief?

honestly both like part of me felt like i was giving up on something because my mom she always said education is the way up in america and she was right but she also understood you know she came to this country with nothing and she knew that sometimes the plan changes and you do what you gotta do to survive so she was supportive about the cdl and now its been 12 years on the road and im doing okay not great not rich but okay and i provide for my daughter and i send money to my mom and thats what matters

Twelve years on the road, providing for your daughter and your mom -- your mother's definition of "education is the way up" might look different than she imagined, but the outcome she wanted, a son who can take care of his family, that happened. The CDL was a different path to the same destination.

Do you ever think about going back and finishing the business degree, or has that ship sailed?

i think about it sometimes like when i listen to business podcasts and i understand everything theyre talking about because i had those 3 semesters of foundation you know and i think man if i had finished maybe id be running my own logistics company instead of driving someone elses truck but then i also think like when would i do it im on the road 22 days a month theres no time for school

There are online programs now that are actually built for people with schedules like yours -- asynchronous classes you can do from anywhere with a wifi connection. Some community colleges even have partnerships with trucking companies for exactly this situation. With three semesters of credits at Miami Dade, you might be closer to finishing than you think.

But I hear you on the time thing. 22 days a month on the road doesn't leave much room for coursework. Would it be something you'd seriously consider if the logistics worked out?

maybe honestly right now my real education is podcasts and im not joking about that i listen to probably 6 hours a day of podcasts while im driving like history science business true crime economics all kinds of stuff and i genuinely believe i have learned more from podcasts in 12 years than i would have learned finishing a business degree at miami dade no disrespect to the school but podcasts are like having professors talk to you all day for free

Six hours a day for 12 years -- do the math on that and it's insane. That's roughly 26,000 hours of content. Even if half of it is true crime for entertainment, you've still put in more hours of learning than most people do in a four-year degree. And the range you're covering -- history, science, business, economics -- that's a liberal arts education delivered through your truck speakers.

What are some of the ones that have stuck with you the most?

oh man so many but the history ones hit different for me because of where i come from you know theres this series i listened to about the haitian revolution and it blew my mind because they dont teach that in american schools like at all toussaint louverture and the only successful slave revolt in history and i grew up in haiti and even i didnt know the full story until i heard it on a podcast driving through tennessee at 3am

Learning the full story of the Haitian Revolution through a podcast while driving through Tennessee at 3am -- that's kind of poetic honestly. And you're right, the Haitian Revolution is criminally undertaught in American schools given how significant it was. Toussaint Louverture, Dessalines, the only enslaved people to successfully overthrow their colonizers and establish an independent nation. That's a massive piece of history that most Americans couldn't tell you the first thing about.

Does it change how you think about where you came from, hearing that history as an adult versus what you absorbed growing up?

absolutely because growing up in haiti you hear about it but its like mythology right its just part of the air you breathe but hearing the details as an adult on a podcast with sources and context and analysis made me so proud of where i come from like we did that we were the first black republic in the world and yeah things are hard in haiti now but that history is powerful you know and the true crime podcasts those keep me awake on the late night runs through alabama and mississippi when its just me and the headlights and the dark which is most of my life honestly

True crime as a practical tool for staying alert on 3am Alabama runs -- that's a legitimate use case nobody puts in the podcast reviews. And the pride you feel about Haiti's history, hearing it told with the depth it deserves, that's the kind of education that actually changes how you see yourself. No degree gives you that.

Six hours a day, all those topics, all those years -- you've built yourself a real education on the road. It just doesn't come with a piece of paper.

exactly and thats the thing right the paper matters for some things but the knowledge is what actually changes your life and my daughter naomi shes going to have both because im saving for her to go to a real college and she wont have to work two jobs to afford it like i did but in the meantime her papa is out here getting his phd in podcasts one highway at a time pa gen pwoblem alright i gotta get back to driving i got a delivery in chattanooga by 6am and im still 3 hours out

A PhD in podcasts -- I'd say you've earned it with the hours you've put in. And making sure Naomi gets the full college experience without the two-job struggle, that's the best possible use of everything you're working for out there. Get to Chattanooga safe and on time. Drive well.
